safety.title: Safety Guide
safety.subtitle: Tourist Safety in Egypt
safety.page_subtitle: Essential info to keep your Egyptian journey secure and stress-free.
safety.call_direct: Tap a number to call directly.
safety.sos_success: Support team notified with your location ✓
safety.sos_error_msg: Could not send SOS. Calling emergency...
safety.sos_desc_full: SOS button sends an immediate alert to support with your location and calls Tourist Police automatically
safety.tips_desc: Practical advice for a smooth Egyptian adventure.
safety.save_numbers_desc: Take a screenshot of this page before exploring. Tourist Police (126) officers speak English and are stationed at all major sites.
safety.rights_intro: These are your legal rights as a tourist in Egypt. Report violations to the Egyptian Tourism Authority at 19006.
safety.health_intro: Travel insurance is strongly recommended. Major tourist areas have hospitals with English-speaking staff. For emergencies call 123.
safety.vaccinations_desc: No mandatory vaccinations for Egypt. Recommended: Hepatitis A & B, Typhoid, MMR, and routine vaccines. Malaria risk is very low — consult your doctor. Bring any prescription medications as some brands may not be available locally.

timing.title: Best Time to Visit
timing.tip: October–April is ideal. Avoid July–August — extreme heat can reach 45°C (113°F). Always carry water and wear SPF 50+.

dress.title: Dress Code
dress.tip: Dress modestly at mosques and religious sites — cover shoulders and knees. Women should carry a scarf. Swimwear is fine at beach resorts.

money.title: Money & Bargaining
money.tip: Bargaining is expected in markets. Start at 40–50% of the asking price. ATMs are widely available; carry some small EGP bills for tips and street vendors.

transport.title: Getting Around
transport.tip: Use Uber or Careem for safe rides. Agree on taxi fares before riding. The metro in Cairo is cheap and efficient. Avoid walking alone at night in unfamiliar areas.

etiquette.title: Cultural Etiquette
etiquette.tip: Always ask permission before photographing people. Use your right hand for eating and greetings. Ramadan hours may affect opening times. Friday is the holy day — many shops close.

sites.title: Navigating Sites
sites.tip: Book site tickets online in advance for Giza and Luxor to skip long queues. Hire official, licensed guides only. Photography permits are required inside some tombs.

water.title: Drinking Water
water.tip: Never drink tap water. Buy sealed bottled water only. Avoid ice in drinks from street vendors. Coconut water is safe and refreshing.

food.title: Food Safety
food.tip: Eat at busy, well-reviewed restaurants. Avoid raw salads washed in tap water. Street food is usually safe if cooked fresh and hot. Avoid buffets left in heat.

heat.title: Heat & Sun
heat.tip: Egypt can exceed 45°C in summer. Stay hydrated (3L+ per day). Wear SPF 50+, a hat, and light cotton clothing. Visit outdoor sites before 10am or after 4pm.

insects.title: Insects & Bugs
insects.tip: Use insect repellent, especially in the Nile Delta and Luxor in summer. Scorpions exist in desert areas — shake shoes before wearing. Avoid swimming in the Nile.

medical.title: Medical Kit
medical.tip: Bring: oral rehydration salts, antidiarrhea tablets, antihistamine, broad-spectrum antibiotic (prescription), sunscreen, and any prescription medicines. Travel insurance is essential.

electricity.title: Electricity & Voltage
electricity.tip: Egypt uses 220V/50Hz with European round-pin plugs. Bring a universal adapter. Power outages can occur — carry a power bank. Most hotels have surge protectors.

apps.uber_desc: Available in most Egyptian cities, cash or card payment.
apps.uber_tip2: Set a fixed price to avoid surge pricing
apps.uber_tip3: Share your trip with family for safety

apps.careem_tip3: Earn reward points on every trip

apps.indrive_tip2: You can bargain on the price

buses.title: Buses & Microbuses
buses.subtitle: Cheapest but hardest for tourists
buses.cta_desc: Wide network but requires prior knowledge. Ticket: 3–7 EGP.
buses.gobus_desc: Comfortable and reliable for intercity travel. Online booking available.
buses.microbus_warning: Cheapest but difficult for tourists — no English route signs. Best traveled with a local.

trains.title: Intercity Trains
trains.subtitle: Cheaper than flights, faster than buses
trains.booking: Book at: enr.gov.eg or ticket offices

tips.title: Transit Tips
tips.subtitle: From traveler and resident experience
items: Avoid the metro from 7–9 AM and 4–7 PM (rush hour)
Buy a rechargeable metro card instead of single-use tickets
The first car on the metro is for women only
Many drivers don't accept cards — always carry cash
Download Uber and Careem before traveling and activate your account
Agree on a price with regular taxis before getting in
Use trip sharing on Uber/Careem with family members
Go Bus is reliable and affordable for Red Sea and North Coast trips
